Long ago, before the towering mountains had names, the West Wind possessed a voice of pure, crystalline melody. It sang through the emerald valleys, coaxing the first blooms of spring from the frostbitten earth. Auralia, a lonely nymph of the rocky peaks, grew deeply jealous of this song. Though she possessed immense beauty and eyes like polished obsidian, she was entirely mute.

One evening, while the West Wind rested in a deep, shadowed cavern, Auralia crept in and stole its voice, binding the magical breath within a hollow silver reed. When she blew into the reed, the most magnificent sounds poured forth, echoing across the world. 

But the ancient mountain gods witnessed her greedy theft. Furious at her vanity and disruption of nature, the gods shattered the silver reed and cursed Auralia. 

"Since you coveted the voices of others," the chief god thundered, the sky trembling, "you shall never have a true voice of your own. You will only ever speak what has already been spoken."

They bound her invisible spirit to the canyon walls. Now, when weary travelers shout into the deep ravines, it is Auralia who answers, doomed to endlessly repeat their final words.

Long ago, before the sky had stars, the night was a heavy, suffocating blanket. The plants wept in the dark, unable to unfurl their leaves, and the animals huddled in fear. Only the Sun possessed light, hoarding it in a great golden chalice on the highest peak of the world.

A humble moth, pale and unnoticed, could not bear the sorrow of the dark. While the mighty eagles and fierce lions cowered, the moth fluttered up the treacherous mountain. The air grew thin and blistering hot, singeing her fragile wings, but she pressed on. When she reached the summit, she found the Sun asleep, his chalice bubbling with liquid brilliance.

Knowing she could not carry the heavy vessel, the moth dipped her tiny body into the glowing nectar. The light seared her, but she drank deep and rolled in the golden dust until she was luminescent. She flew back down, shaking herself over the forests and fields. Wherever a drop of sunlight fell from her wings, it took life, becoming a firefly.

The moth perished from the terrible heat, but her glowing children remain today, tiny sparks of the stolen sun, dancing to keep the encroaching darkness forever at bay.

Myth: Long ago, the world was divided between the scorching reign of the Sun and the absolute, freezing void of the Night. There were no stars, only an endless, heavy black. Lumina, a pale silver fox of the snowy tundra, loved the quiet of the dark but pitied the creatures who froze during the Sun’s absence.

Determined to bring warmth to the shadows, Lumina climbed the celestial staircase while the Sun dozed. She did not seek a mere drop of his light, but attempted to steal his entire crown of embers. As she clamped her jaws around the glowing circlet, the blistering heat scorched her mouth, but she bounded down the steps.

Awakened by the theft, the furious Sun struck Lumina with a spear of pure daylight. The crown shattered in her jaws, scattering a million tiny, glowing fragments across the obsidian sky. These embers became the stars, offering gentle guidance in the dark.

As punishment for her thievery, the Sun bound Lumina to the sky itself. Now, she wanders the night as the Moon, changing shape as she eternally hunts for the scattered remnants of the stolen crown, glowing only with the echoes of the light she once touched.
